title: Workbook — Community: Living the Faith Together
html:
Student Workbook

To use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. Why does the Catechism say that “the human person needs to live in society”? (CCC 1879)
  2. What is the importance of the fourth commandment for social life?
  3. What are the three elements of the common good according to the Catechism? (CCC 1907-1909)
  4. What is the principle of subsidiarity? Why is it important?
  5. What is solidarity according to Saint John Paul II? (CCC 1939)
  6. When does a citizen have the right and duty to conscientious objection? (CCC 2242)

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no “right” answers to these personal questions.

1. Which community has shaped you most deeply? Describe a specific moment when that community sustained you or transformed you.

It could be your family, a parish group, school friends, a movement…

 

 

 

2. How are you living the fourth commandment today? Is there something you need to do—a call, a visit, a reconciliation—towards your parents?

If your parents have passed away, consider how to honor their memory. If the relationship is difficult, think of a possible first step.

 

 

 

3. Who has been the “Samaritan” who helped you when you were left on the road? Give that person a name and a face. And you: whom are you called to be a Samaritan to today?

Remember the parable: the Samaritan asked nothing. He simply acted.

 

 

 

4. In which area of your life do you tend toward individualism? Where do you tell yourself: “I manage on my own, I don’t need anyone”?

Faith, work, emotional difficulties, important decisions… What would happen if you opened that area to the community?

 

 

 

5. The “consistent ethic of life” calls us to defend every human life. Is there any group of people whose dignity you find difficult to recognize or defend? Why?

The unborn, immigrants, the elderly, prisoners, addicts, people who think differently from you…

 

 

 

6. What can you do this week to strengthen the Christian community to which you belong?

Be concrete: a call, a visit, volunteering for something, reconciling with someone, inviting someone to Mass…

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Map of My Community

Draw on a sheet of paper a diagram with concentric circles. In the center, put your name. In the first circle: your immediate family. In the second: your extended family and close friends. In the third: your parish or faith group. In the fourth: your local community (neighbors, coworkers or schoolmates). Next to each person or group, write a word that expresses what you receive from them (“support,” “joy,” “faith,” “advice”…). Then, next to that, write what you can give them. This is your communion map. Take care of it.

 

 

 

B. The Letter I Haven’t Written

Write a letter to your parents (or to one of them). If they are alive, you can give it to them. If not, keep it as a prayer. In the letter, thank them for three specific things they gave you. Ask forgiveness for something specific where you failed them. And tell them something you have never told them. It doesn’t have to be long; it has to be true.

 

 

 

C. A Door to Open

Like during the posadas, this week open a door. It could be literal (inviting someone to your home) or symbolic (approaching someone from whom you had distanced yourself, welcoming someone new to your group, listening to someone you normally ignore). Write here which door you will open and with whom.

 

 

 

Verse to Memorize

“All the believers were together and had everything in common. Selling their possessions and goods, they gave to anyone as he had need.”

— Acts of the Apostles 2:44-45

Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus,
who chose to be born into a family,
to live in community, and to die for all:
teach me to live the faith together.
Heal my individualism and my pride,
open my eyes to the brother and sister who suffer,
and give me a Samaritan heart,
that does not ask who deserves help
but stops and acts.
May my family, my parish, my community
be a reflection of your Trinitarian love.
May I never forget that I am not saved alone,
but with you and with my brothers and sisters.
Amen.

Print Workbook
